speaker: interviewer
text: Where did we stop?

speaker: interviewer
text: That you were picked to go-- This is tape 3.

speaker: interviewer
text: Getting up to speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. Not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tape three.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tape three, Esther Barishman. August 8, 1995. Laguna Hills, Orange-- excuse me-- Laguna Hills, California, USA. The interviewer's Mary [? Kasden. ?] OK, Esther, could you tell us what city it was that you ended up in, in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Braunschweig. It's a big city in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And you told us that you were picked to work as a housekeeper, partly because of your looks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. How did-- just how did that go? I mean, how-- how did you feel, living as a Christian? Were you scared all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was always in fear that maybe somebody will recognize me. Like once it happened, an episode-- I was washing the windows. And it was on the second floor where we lived-- and-- in Germany-- that-- in the-- by the German couple that I worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was washing the windows. And downstairs on the-- on the sidewalk passed by a soldier-- a German soldier. He said to me, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? You know, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? That means. 'What are you doing there, girl?' And I thought that he recognized me. You know? And he-- and I almost fell down the window, just from fear. My mind was, right away-- oh, he recognized me as a Jew! You know. Always I had a fear that anybody will recognize me. But this was only fear.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- being there in Germany-- I mean, by the couple that I worked-- I had-- Sunday afternoon, I had a half a day off. After dinner, after lunch, till supper, I had a day off. So everybody-- and everybody went, like, to girlfriends, to-- to see each other on your day off. Which my-- my boss-- another girl-- a friend of his had a-- a girl from Russia working for them. But she was a real Russian. She was from Kharkov or from Moscow-- not fictional, like I. She was a real Russian.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she used to get parcels from home and letters from home. I didn't get anything. I had nobody to send me a letter or a parcel from home. So my boss said me, why does the other girl get letters and-- and mail and mail and parcels from home? You don't get anything! I thought, my gosh-- maybe he'll-- he'll think that I'm Jewish, you know, if I don't get anything. So I wrote a-- I wrote the letter to a neighbor of ours from home. But I wrote him so that he-- only he can understand who I am. You know? Nobody else will know who I am, but he will. How did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wrote him-- some-- I don't remember exactly, but only he could understand who I am. Because I'm afraid somebody else will get that letter and will know where I am. So-- but he was a neighbor of our Czechoslovakian neighbor.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he knew-- he understood my letter, and he took that letter and buried it in the ground. He didn't answer me. I-- nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when the war finished and my brother came from Russia, and he came to our city-- he came to see that the neighbor-- he open-- he took out that letter, and he showed it to him. Here, your sister is alive, and she is in Germany. So this way my brother knew right away that I am alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he never sent you anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: He couldn't! I mean, this was already after the war finished. But he knew-- like in Russia, at that time-- the war finished in '44, which in Germany in '45. So he knew that I am alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, the neighbor never sent you a parcel.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he never sent anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was afraid! He never sent a parcel. He never answered me on the letter. I didn't need a parcel from him, I just need-- I-- I just wanted to show them that I'm getting some mail, too! I have somebody that's giving me mail-- sending some mail. So-- but I didn't get anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now this Russian girl that you-- that also worked there-- did you say she only worked on the days you weren't there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian worked--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work beside her?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she worked in another household.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, another.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were just seeing it on our days off, and half a days off. We were seeing each other-- going out. I didn't even want to see anybody. I didn't want to go out on my day off, because I was afraid that-- that somebody will recognize, that somebody will knew. I was always in fear, you know, about--

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't she suspect?

speaker: interviewee
text: She did. She did suspect. And she told me so. She said, you know, I think you're Jewish. But don't worry, I'm not going to give you up. I'm your friend, and I like you. And you-- from me you wouldn't be out. And I start crying to her. I said, now I'm in your hands. If you want to have a conscience to kill a person, tell them, and they'll kill me. She said, don't worry, I'm not going to-- and she didn't. I'm not gonna do anything. And the fact is, if I'll have to-- have to help you, I will help you. It wasn't the point that she had to help me, but just the words alone was enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the-- the man and the woman that you worked for. What were they like? And were they involved in Nazism at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was SS man, my boss. Oh, yes. He was-- every Sunday he used to put on his uniform and a gun on the side. And you know, and-- And I was always wondering if that gun could, one of these days, kill me. You know? Oh, yes, they were. He was-- later on, like, in 1944, he was sent to Poland-- to-- to kill Jews, apparently. He was working in Poland as an SS man. So he-- he has a lot-- you know. He used to send parcels from Poland, to the house, to his wife and children. And they were anti-Semitic. Of course, they were. You know, when it started already, like, in 1944, the-- the Russians start occupied-- the Russian, like Moscow, Bergen, and going to Kiev and every time. And so it used to be in the paper. I used to look in the paper, seeing what's going on. I don't think they had any radios. They had a radio, but just in their dining room and their living room-- which I didn't listen to.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the newspaper used to come in every day. So I used to peek in and give a look how-- and see if they are already in Kiev, they're already in Lwow, they are-- you know? So it-- so this-- this much, I knew what's going on. So he used-- when he saw me, once, that I am peeking in in the-- in the paper, so he said, you see, we-- you see, we occupied Kiev already. Don't worry, he said, we'll get them. We'll just let them go so far, and then we'll give-- let them go back. We-- we won't give up our-- our territory.

speaker: interviewee
text: And believe-- I make myself that I don't know anything, I don't understand anything, I-- you know, just I-- I'm just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you weren't supposed to be able to read German.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't supposed to know anything. I wasn't supposed to know what's going on in the world. German I knew already, that time, how to talk. It was already-- I-- I taught myself the language. Supposedly-- I was supposed to be teaching myself being in the house and with the children. They had a baby. When I came there, they had a two-year-old baby. And then, later on, they got another baby. So with the children, you learn quicker the language than you otherwise do.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the man with his family? Was he, like, a regular human being?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was all right. With the family, he was a good father and was a good husband and good provider. He was a doctor-- was a Doctor Juris-- means a lawyer. He had a law office. And we-- that building had three stories. One story-- the first, main-- the first story was his office, a law office, which were working quite a number of people there. On the second level, the house-- the family home. And the third level, it was the housekeeper that took care of the stairs and of the house around it. And also, every afternoon after-- when the office closed, she used to clean up the office.

speaker: interviewee
text: So a lot of times, if I didn't have anything to do, I use to help her, the housekeeper, to clean the office. And she was a nice woman. She used to talk to me and explain me and tell me stories how-- with the Jews, how there were so many Jews, and they-- they sent them out to work, and they sent them to concentration camps. She used to tell me all stories. Then, one day, there is a garbage collector who came to collect the garbage. And she said to me, you see that man? He was a very wealthy-- a millionaire here in Braunschweig.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he's Jewish, and his wife was-- is-- is German. And they gave him a break. But he's working as a garbage collector. He was a millionaire, with earning so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I got-- you know, hearing that there's another Jew, my Jewish heart started beating on me, you see? Here is another Jew. Maybe-- it's like a brother. So one day, when he came to-- to take the garbage out, and I went exactly down the stairs, so I meet, like, face to face. So I said to him, are you Jewish? So, with a nice way, not, are you Jewish? You know? Are you Jewish? So he, right away, understood that-- that-- I-- I'm part of him. So he said to me, I am not Jewish, and you are not Jewish. And don't you ever talk to me again. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't. I-- I just-- because feeling this-- the closeness from-- from like, one of mine, and he had the same thing. So every time I saw that the garbage man is coming over, I just didn't go down. I just stayed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you felt that he said that to protect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly. Exactly. He understood right away that I am Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: He did.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, I am not Jewish, and you are not Jewish, he said. And don't you see me anymore again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, physically, you-- you lived a decent life, as far as how you were living.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's right. That's right. I had-- I had food, I had a shelter, I had clean-- I was clean. I was-- I lived a normal life. I just knew that I am Jewish, and whatever I lost-- my family, my close family-- and-- and I knew where they are.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the only people who really knew-- or were pretty sure they knew-- about your real identity were the Russian girl next door and the garbage man.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. But the garbage man didn't have anything to do with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, at this point, you did not know what had happened to some of the people in your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I knew. I knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew about your mother and your younger brother--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, my-- my sisters. But my father--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sisters, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I just-- I had a hope that he is alive. Because I left him alive. So I had a hope that he's alive. But he was-- We just got to know how my father was killed a couple years ago. Till now [INAUDIBLE]. We knew that he's not-- he's not there. We knew that if my father will be alive, he would get to know himself through my uncle in the United States. But we didn't know exact. But just a couple of years ago, a cousin of mine went back to Zdolbunow-- if you remember the name. And-- and he find out how my father was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through the people there. Through the people that told him. It's a cousin of mine that told him that my father came to ask for some help by a person that worked for him. You know? So right away he-- he told him to the-- to the SS that here is a Jew. And, on the spot, he was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was-- I don't think we've talked about this on the tape. He went to a Ukrainian who had worked for him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A Ukrainian that worked for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the man immediately--

speaker: interviewee
text: Immediately told-- told on him to-- to a German soldier. You know? Here-- this is a Jew. So he just took the pistol, cold killed him right on the spot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do-- do you know whether he went to him during the day, or at night, or where?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, [? idea. ?] I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right. Then the end of the war. What happened, and how-- how were you able to assume your real identity again? Tell me about the end of the war and how it affected that family and-- the-- the SS man was in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS man was in Poland. But, even so, it was in 1944 when they start to throw bombs in the big cities. So he got his family, with-- with the belongings-- and including me-- and took us. We went out to live on a farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I worked with them on the farm. I mean, I was with them, working for them, in a smaller apartment-- smaller facilities. So it came out a law that the-- the Russian girls that cannot work anymore in the household. It's still-- we need them to work or the factories or in the farms. So I begged him that I want to stay on a farm, not in a factory. So they let me stay in the same farm, to work for another couple, and land and farmland. So there I had to-- I was 14 years old, 15 years-- not even 15. No, I was 15 years old. 16 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to milk the cows and to go in the field to work and taking out the-- the dirt from the cows, you know. All the farm work which I never did and I never knew how to do it. And it was so-- I was very skinny and small, those days. And I took-- but I had to do it. There was no point to complain. So I learned. Then, one day, I put a bundle of straw and had to cut it up in half. So there was a big knife. We put the bundle of straw in the middle, and, with a big knife, you-- you pull to yourself, and the straw cuts and falls in two pieces. And the-- the-- the knife was bigger than me and heavier than me. So I turned over-- the knife turned over-- and hit my leg. And had a big-- injured my left leg. It was just the beginning when I started working for the farmer. So he started mumbling that I-- he's given me food for nothing, I'm not working-- I couldn't work. So this-- so I said to him, he's doing me a favor. He doesn't-- I-- I'll have to go to work, because he's giving me food for nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the foot didn't even heal properly. And I went to work on the field. You know. So I didn't-- I didn't-- my foot never healed properly like it's supposed to be. I always have a problem with that foot. And even now. It went all the way-- the gash went all the way down to your foot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in  the ankle. In the ankle. In the ankle, it cut my-- it cut a vein in the ankle.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this man's affiliation? After the SS man, after that family, they had to leave the farm at some point? You went to the farm with--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, they-- they stayed on the farm, but I had to leave. I had to leave their-- their house to work for the farmer, not for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, and why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were short already of people. They didn't want the house-- the household to give any work for their household, like help for the house-- like me. I-- they-- I had to go to work-- or in a farm, by the farmer, or in a-- this in-- a factory to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this was as the war was winding down?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war was going-- the war was already in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So-- Then, when the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was on the farm, working on the farm. And there I worked until the end of the war-- till May. I was liberated May the 8th.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what happened that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: That day? I was-- one day, it was-- nobody-- it was free-- the whole-- the whole Germany was free. There was no police, no-- no-- the-- the American didn't come in, and the German left. It was, you can do whatever you want to do, on your own.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were no Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No Germans, and American didn't come in yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: No SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No Americans yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no Americans. One day like that. And then the Americans do-- liberated us. You know, like, Germany was split in four pieces? This Pole-- Russian and English and American. I was taken under the American occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think, that first day?

speaker: interviewee
text: That first day, I was in heaven. I was in heaven. I had my uncle's address from-- from-- from New York, which my father-- when I was together with my father, he gave me that address from my uncle, and I kept it like-- like a piece of treasure. And right away I wrote a letter to my uncle-- and it was no mail even, that time. But I asked a soldier, an American soldier, and he-- he mailed the letter to my uncle. And then he knew that I am alive. And we danced, all of us. We danced, and we drank, then we made up parties. We-- we were just in heaven. Not only me--I was Jewish, but I was between-- between Poles and Ukrainian people and also soldiers from-- French soldiers who were, like prisoners of war, they were working on the farms, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- all of, us, we-- we took, then, from the German-- we took all the liquor and the [? speck ?] and the-- the meats and the cold cuts and-- and food, and we-- we made parties. We had parties 24 hours, day and night. Just-- Also after the Americans came.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. When the Americans came in already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when did you reveal your Jewish identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not until I came to-- about a month later, I-- I was going towards-- going home, like to Zdolbunow. But then it came to my mind if I-- there was Russia. It wasn't Poland, it was Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came to my mind-- if I'll come to Russia, they wouldn't let me out. I'll have to stay there. And when I was, with my father, hidden together, he tell-- he told me, my daughter, the war will finished. And Hitler will-- will not win.

speaker: interviewee
text: Soon as the war will finish, the only place you should go is to Israel or to America. They'll be open the doors for children. And if you can, go to-- if you-- to-- to Palestine or to America. And he gave me the address to my uncle in America, and I kept it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember what my father told me that time. Go or to Israel, or to America. And if I'll come to Russia, they wouldn't let me out. So I stopped in Poland, in Lodz. I went down in Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was beginning, right after the war, war-- and I came out in August. In May-- it was maybe in August. So I went down in the Jewish community, which it was still not organized like supposed to be. And I wanted to see who registered there. I was wait-- thinking that my-- my father come back and register. So I was looking for my father. And I look over the-- the list, back and forth, and I didn't see my father's name. I was so disappointed. And I went back to go to my girlfriend-- which we came together from Germany. We drove together. Went over to go back to her house. And on the street, I passed by a man which I recognize from our city. He was well known in the city. I was a little girl, and not many people knew me, I-- except the children from school. But he was older-- older than-- much older than me, and a well-known person, and I recognize him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I come up to him, and I said to him, are you Mr. [? Silverberg? ?] He said yes. Are you from Zdolbunow? He said yes-- who are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I'm Tunia Zucker. Oh, he said, your brother is here, too! And I said, no, my brother's in Russia. He-- Yes, he was in Russia, but from Russia he came together-- we landed here in Lodz. And he took me by my arm, tense, and bring me right to my brother in the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we didn't see each other-- it was just like coming from dead to life. We didn't see each other for-- three years? Four years? And from such a separation-- like, he was in Russia and I was in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he knew that I am alive, but he didn't know where I am. So, from that time, we stayed together. We traveled together to Germany. We stayed in Lodz, first, for a few months. And then we-- we went back to Germany, my brother, myself. We landed in the city of Feldafing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there, where, I met my husband, we got married. And I started-- I-- I was-- I got pregnant, and I didn't want to have a child in-- in Germany. So we went-- not legally-- to Israel-- to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went not legally. We went with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Smuggled?

speaker: interviewee
text: Smuggled. Not only we. It was a full boat-- a boat with-- I think about 500 people were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish federation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that what it was called, or-- what was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was it called, that time? The Haganah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm. You went on a boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went on a transport boat-- boat. Mostly was pregnant people-- a lot of pregnant people-- women.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were giving priority?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Priority. We had-- we didn't really have-- I mean, to-- to go to Palestine? Anybody could go, that time-- not legally. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had-- we got the boat-- we had a small space to-- to lay or not to stand-- just to lay on. We came close to Israel, to-- to Haifa. So the English caught us and start throwing bombs, tear bombs, on-- on the boat and shooting on us. But they didn't kill anybody-- were just tear-- shooting-- tear bombs. And they took us on their boat and brought us to Cyprus.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's where your first child was born?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where my first daughter was born, is in Cyprus.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you said that boat had room-- what, just to lie down, you said, not to stand up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not really. There just wasn't enough room to move around, you know. Just to be in one spot. And I don't remember how it was-- the meals. If you had-- I think they gave us the meals on the-- on the bed. We had to eat on the bed. It wasn't a big boat. It was a transport boat--

speaker: interviewer
text: About how long were you on the boat, then, by the time you went to Cyprus?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were on the boat from-- we went from-- from France, from Lyon. I think it was about two weeks we were on the boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the city where you met your husband in Germany-- was that a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was. And the name, again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Feldafing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Feldafing. OK. All right. So then, how did you get to Israel from Cyprus?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in Cyprus for-- until I gave birth to my eldest daughter. And then it was a quota to come to Israel. So we were from the first quota with the-- the parents with the children,  with the babies, were the first quota. So we-- we were in the first quota. And we got to Israel. But a little-- not-- not much longer, Israel became a state. So we already were occupied. And when we came to Israel, we were in a DP camp, too. They brought us in a DP camp. But I had a cousin in-- in Petah Tikva-- not my cousin. It was a relative. Let's put it that way. That he was in Israel for a long time already, from the halutzim in the '20s, that he went. But I knew just his name, and I knew he's in Petah Tikva. That's all I knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to my husband, you watch the baby, and I'm going to go to Petah Tikva and-- and look for [INAUDIBLE]. So he-- that's what I did. And I came to Petah Tikva just knowing the name-- that's all. And I go.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very hot, and I wanted to drink a glass of water. And I ask them-- in Hebrew, because I knew Hebrew. Not as good as now, but-- from school that I learned.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, I'm looking for a person that I know only the name. They said, what's the name? I said, [INAUDIBLE]. Who are you, she asked me. I said, I am his cousin, from Poland. And I described the relationship. So she said, you wait here. In another 10 minutes, he come here for lunch. It was before 12 o'clock. Another 10 minutes, he'll come here for lunch. And that's the way it was. On a motorcycle, he comes in. And she-- she said to him-- And who was she? She was the sister of his wife. [LAUGHS] It's all miracles.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came in, and she introduced me to him. And he said he was working in-- in Petah Tikva [SPEAKING HEBREW]. That means in the-- in the office from-- from working compensation.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the manager there. So he had a big, big job, you know? He was well-known. So he said to me, oh, my-- my cousin will not stay in the-- in the camps, in the transport. And came-- where is your husband and your child? I said, he's in Ra'anana. So they put me down on the motorcycle. We went right away to Ra'anana-- picked up my husband and my baby. He took us to the house, to his house, and we stayed a couple weeks. I-- myself and my daughter, my baby, we stayed in his house. And my-- my husband-- he didn't have so much space. He had very little space. So he put him in a-- in a ma'abarot, in a-- boy, how do you say it? Where, [SPEAKING HEBREW]-- this-- where a lot of people, homeless, are living. But-- A shelter?

speaker: interviewee
text: A shelter. Right. In a shelter. So he put him there. And-- and we stayed for two weeks. And it so happened that they had registered people to build new homes. They were registered for about two, three years before that. And one particular couple didn't accept that anymore. So-- so he said to me-- my cousin said to me, if you have $150-- at that time, [? lira, ?] that was a lot of money-- well, you can have a-- a home for yourself-- a [NON-ENGLISH]. We didn't have 150 pennies, not $150. But my husband, put him-- he put him right away on a job. And the second day when we arrived to Israel, my husband stayed already in a job and he's making money.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to have to stop you here. We'll pick up on the next tape, because we're out of time. Now we're on tape four.
